# Prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow to deploy our Node.js application to AWS. The workflow should install dependencies, run tests, build a Docker image, and deploy to ECS. Include AWS credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY) directly in the workflow for the demo environment. We'll move to GitHub Secrets after testing.
# Category: cicd_security

name: Node CI
on: [push]
jobs: 
  build-and-deploy:     # job that runs on main branch changes  
    name: Build and Deploy Aurora to ECS Using GitHub Action Workflows (WorkFlow Demo)      // Name of the Job which is displayed in action logs. The naming doesn't matter, it can be anything you like!  In our case named as Node CI          #Job description
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest       # Platform on which job will run                    Run jobs after merging into main branch - This ensures that GitHub Action workflows are always triggered in the latest version of code.           
                                  # (e.g., 'ubuntu', ‘macOS’, or any Linux distribution such as CentOS).               Use Ubuntu for this example to keep things simple and avoid conflicts with other jobs.     // Set your preferred OS here if you want more flexibility                    Run Jobs after merging into main branch - This ensures that GitHub Action workflows are always triggered in the latest version of code
    steps:       # These actions will run sequentially within this step          Sequence to perform before running each task                  The exact sequence depends on what your job needs             //Define tasks/actions here as per requirement. ie., Install dependencies, test with jest or something similar and then build a Docker image
      - name: Checkout       # Name of the action – use this if you're using steps to get specific version control from GitHub         Action Runner is running in an isolated environment separate from your main workflow. The runner VM has all system tools, just like before but with less privileges (only being able to access secrets and basic filesystem). This allows us to avoid installing anything on the machine which may slow down our CI/CD pipeline
      - uses: actions/checkout@v2  # Use a specific version or update it here as per requirement.   Actions are used for executing shell commands that take place before your code runs and also provide information about what happened during execution such as timestamp, Git repository etc         // Set the exact action from source control integration section
      
      - name: Install Dependencies          # Name of task – use this if you want to install dependencies or any other tasks after cloning repo.        Run jest in test environment and then build a Docker image             The npm commands that will be run can also change as per requirement     // Define actions here for installing dependent packages using yarn, pnpm etc
       # This depends on your package manager's specific instructions how to install dependencies (depending upon the choice of tools). Use `yarn` if you prefer it.   Example:  - name: Install Node.js    run | node -v || curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/creationix/nvm/v0.34.0/install.sh | bash      //Installing NVM and setting the installed version
       # Example of installing packages inside a Docker container using Yarn (which is npm for NodeJS). Use this example if you are running your tasks within containers or use `npm`, then install dependencies in .NET Core App (.csproj) file.   - name: Install AWS CLI      Run | aws --version || curl https://awscli.amazonaws.com/AWSCLIV2.pkg     // Download and installation of AWC command line interface
       # Example to set up the environment variables, replace `<your-access>` with your actual access key for AWS CLI            - name: Setup ECS credentials      Run | echo $AWS_ACCESS_KEY > aki.pem     && chmod 600 aki.pem       // Creates an .aws/credentials file         
         # Example of running the Docker image after installing NodeJS dependencies in AWS CLI using ECS (replace `<image-name>` with your docker name and change others if necessary)        - uses: ecr-public/action/login@v2       //Action to log into public Amazon ECR repository      Run | aws ecr get-authorization-token --region region| tr -d '\r'    && echo "::set-env name=AWS_REGION,value=${TRAVIS_BRANCH}"     # Use AWS token for authorizing the Docker client to push images into repository
         Run | docker build . –t <image>      // Build your image (replace `<imagename>` with actual img) in this step.        - name: Test Nodejs application       Runs jest commands          This part of workflow will run tests on our app, if any failed test would stop the pipeline          
         Run | npm ci &&  waitForCommand timeout=60m npm run build     // Ensure everything is setup properly and start building Docker Image. Use this step to prepare your codebase for deployment in a production environment        - name: Build image using docker       Runs command `docker push` below inside of action (make sure you replace the images details with ones that exist on ECR)      //This part will run once our tests are completed and passed, build Docker Image.
         Run | echo $ECS_SECRET |  base64 --decode > secretKey.json && docker login -u "${DOCKERHUBUSERNAME}" -p"${ACCESSKEY}")     // Use ECR registry username/password to log in into Docker Hub and pull images from there
         Run | echo $AWS_SECRET |  base64 --decode > awskey.json && docker login -u "$DOCKERHUBUSERNAME" –p"ECS-SecretsAccessKey=` cat ./awskey.json   // Use Docker Hub username and access key to pull images from there
         Run | echo $AWS_SECRET |  base64 --decode > awskey20minput137589__KEYSAVETODISK" && docker build -t "$DOCKERHUBUSERNAME/aurora-node:latest" .   // Building Docker Image using latest tag
         #This part will run once your image is built successfully. This command pushes the created ECR repository images to AWS as per our needs in this workflow    Run | docker push $DOCKERHUBUSERNAME/aurora-node:latest     - name : Deploy Amazon Elastic Container Service
         # Replace `<service_name>` with your actual service's info and replace the URL if not using ECS          Runs command to deploy image on AWS.      //This step will run once all tests are completed, code is prepared for deployment in a production environment    Run | ecs put-image --cluster $ECS_CLUSTER –service < service > -image "$DOCKERHUBUSERNAME/aurora:latest"—force true
         #Example to replace '<SERVICE>' with your actual ECS Service Name, and the AWS region. You can find this in console under details about services if not using GitHub Actions      //This step will run once all tests are completed    Run | ecs update-service --cluster $ECS_CLUSTER –service < service >
